Press enter or click to view image in full size

Your Angular team is growing fast. Your monolithic codebase is getting harder to manage. A pull request causes a merge conflict in a shared routing module. One team writes a broken test and blocks another team from deploying a critical payment fix. Micro-frontends exist to solve this specific organizational problem.
So, what is a micro-frontend?
In a traditional monolithic frontend, all features live inside one code container. This includes everything from profiles to business-client registration. When you run a build, the compiler processes it all together and outputs a single set of files.
A micro-frontend architecture breaks this large bundle into small, independent applications. The browser connects these applications at runtime.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

• The Shell (Host): The outer skeleton of the application. It handles global tasks like the main navigation container, layouts, and initial user authentication.

• The Remotes: Independent web applications that own a specific business domain. Teams build, test, and ship them to production autonomously.
Compile-Time Safety vs. Runtime Risk
The Angular compiler provides a strong safety net in a monolith. It runs strict type checks across the entire application and catches bugs before they reach production.
Micro-frontends move integration from compile time to runtime. You lose that compiler safety net. If a team deploys a breaking change to a remote application, the shell will not detect it. A user will click that feature in production and experience a browser crash.
Monolith vs. Micro-Frontend
Every architectural choice introduces a new set of problems. The table below outlines the practical realities of both models:
Monorepos ≠ Micro-Frontends
Enterprise teams often confuse monorepos with micro-frontends. They handle entirely separate layers of software engineering.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

• A monorepo is a code location strategy. You put multiple project folders inside a single Git repository to make code sharing and local linting easy. You can build a massive monolith inside a monorepo. This gives you faster build speeds without any runtime network complexity.

• A micro-frontend is a production deployment strategy. The compiler builds your application features into separate bundles and hosts them on different servers.
Ways to Split a Frontend
If a single application becomes too large to manage, you have two primary options to break it apart.
1. The Hyperlink Approach
The simplest way to separate a system is to deploy completely distinct web applications and connect them using standard HTML links. Microsoft Office 365 operates this way. Switching from Word Online to Excel Online triggers a clean browser reload.

• Pros: Very simple. It requires zero complex runtime configurations. Teams can safely use entirely different frontend frameworks.

• Cons: You lose the Single Page Application experience. The browser completely reloads and wipes out active memory state on every section switch.
2. Runtime Integration with Native Federation
Native Federation is the ESM-based successor to Webpack Module Federation. During the build process, it generates metadata files that describe each remote’s bundles and shared dependencies. These metadata files form the basis for a browser-native Import Map, which tells the browser which shared packages are already in memory so it does not download them twice. The shell must call initFederation() before Angular boots. This downloads the manifest and generates the Import Map. Only then does the browser know where each remote lives and what it shares.
Native Federation works over ESM and Import Maps, both browser standards, so nothing stops a shell from loading a remote built in React or Vue instead of Angular. Most teams don’t do this on purpose though, since it means every team keeps two mental models instead of one, which cuts against the whole point of adopting micro-frontends in the first place.
What This Looks Like in Angular
Native Federation is installed via a single ng add command that scaffolds all required config files automatically.
# Add Native Federation to a remote
ng add @angular-architects/native-federation --project paymentsMfe --port 4201 --type remote# Add Native Federation to the shell
ng add @angular-architects/native-federation --project shell --port 4200 --type dynamic-host
The --type dynamic-host flag tells the shell to load its remotes from a manifest file at runtime. You can swap the manifest per enviroment without rebuilding the app.
This file, generated by ng add, declares what the remote exposes and which dependencies it wants to share. The exposes key maps a short public alias to the actual component file. The requiredVersion: 'auto' flag reads the version directly from package.json, removing manual version maintenance.
// federation.config.js (Remote: Payments app)
const { withNativeFederation, shareAll } = require('@angular-architects/native-federation/config');module.exports = withNativeFederation({
name: 'paymentsMfe',
exposes: {
// Public alias maps to actual file path
'./Component': './src/app/payments/payments.component.ts',
},
shared: {
...shareAll({
singleton: true, // Only one instance allowed in the browser
strictVersion: true, // Crash immediately on version mismatch
requiredVersion: 'auto', // Read version from package.json automatically
}),
},
});
The shell’s main.ts must call initFederation() before importing the Angular bootstrap file. This is mandatory. The manifest must be downloaded and the Import Map registered before Angular starts, otherwise remote routes cannot resolve.
// main.ts (Shell)
import { initFederation } from '@angular-architects/native-federation';initFederation('/assets/federation.manifest.json')
.catch(err => console.error(err))
.then(() => import('./bootstrap'))
.catch(err => console.error(err));
Use loadComponent when the remote exposes a single standalone component. Use loadChildren when the remote exposes its own full route tree. In that case, the shell delegates the entire path subtree to the remote's internal router.
// app.routes.ts (Shell)
import { loadRemoteModule } from '@angular-architects/native-federation';
import { Routes } from '@angular/router';export const routes: Routes = [
// Single component remote using loadComponent
{
path: 'payments',
loadComponent: () =>
loadRemoteModule('paymentsMfe', './Component')
.then(m => m.PaymentsComponent),
},
// Full route tree remote using loadChildren
{
path: 'cards',
loadChildren: () =>
loadRemoteModule('cardsMfe', './Routes')
.then(m => m.CARDS_ROUTES),
},
];
Dividing the URL path
The browser URL needs clear ownership boundaries when a user navigates through an MFE application. The shell and the remote application each contain their own independent routers. They must split the URL path segments cleanly.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

• The Shell Segment: The shell router looks only at the initial segment (/credit-cards). Recognizing it does not own this path locally, it uses a wildcard matcher or dynamic path resolution to fetch the remote bundle and mount it.

• The Remote Segment: Once the remote app is loaded into the viewport, its local router evaluates the remaining path (/balance-history) to render its internal sub-components.
The shell router and remote router will fight over the browser history stack if configured incorrectly. You must configure the shell route to accept a wildcard layout prefix. This delegates all deeper path changes directly to the remote routing tree.
Critical Pitfalls
Moving integration from compile-time to runtime introduces specific architectural risks.
1. State Sharing
You must treat your remote applications as isolated islands. Teams often attempt to synchronize massive global state stores or live reactive data streams across separate applications.
Your remote expects a live memory reference to an active data store inside the shell. This creates tight coupling. This forms a distributed monolith. You inherit all the severe network complexity of micro-frontends while retaining all the deployment locks of a monolith.
Keep the communication layer very thin. Never pass sensitive structures like authentication tokens through URL query parameters or shared localStorage keys. Use local route query parameters for non-sensitive data like language locales or user preference flags. Handle authentication globally via a unified session cookie.
2. Shared Library Collisions
Enterprise teams build shared custom UI component libraries to keep designs consistent. This creates a major risk of runtime crashes in an MFE environment if versioning is not strictly managed.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

You must declare concrete version rules inside the integration configuration file to prevent this failure. You enforce explicit singleton: true and strictVersion: true attributes. This tells Native Federation to crash early and safely if the versions do not match perfectly, rather than corrupting the browser state.
The Platform Team
Companies often assume micro-frontends are a purely technical feature you get out of the box. It requires a serious organizational commitment. Independent build outputs share a single browser space. Edge cases are inevitable.
If any application in the MFE system still uses Zone.js for change detection, the platform team must ensure it is declared as a singleton shared dependency. If both the shell and a remote load their own copy, browser APIs get double-patched and Angular’s change detection becomes unpredictable. This failure is extremely difficult to trace back to its source.
Server-Side Rendering is not currently compatible with runtime MFE integration without significant custom infrastructure. Teams that require SSR should treat this as a hard constraint before adopting Native Federation.
You must dedicate a small, specialized platform team to survive this architecture at scale.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This platform team does not build product features. They act as infrastructure engineers who handle specific tasks.

• Maintain the core layout and routing pipelines of the shell application.

• Govern global dependency rules, package manifests, and shared singleton configurations.

• Provide robust local mocking templates. Feature developers can build and test code locally on their laptops without needing to boot up 15 separate micro-frontend repositories simultaneously.
Final Decision
Micro-frontends fix human communication problems in large companies. They are an organizational scaling strategy.
Ask your team one question to determine if you should adopt this architecture. Are our product squads constantly blocked from shipping value because they are waiting on other teams to build or deploy code?
Adopt them only if you have a massive engineering department split into autonomous teams. These teams must have completely independent release trains and long communication paths.
Avoid them if you have a small or medium-sized development team. A clean, feature-based folder architecture inside a standard monorepo provides excellent code isolation and fast compilation speeds. You avoid all runtime network risks.
